1: A direct connection: 'Again I saw all the oppressions that are done under the sun. And behold, the tears of the oppressed, and they had no one to comfort them! On the side of their oppressors there was power, and there was no one to comfort them.' The no-comforter for the oppressed — the most grievous deficit Qohelet identifies. John 14:16, 26: 'I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Helper [Parakletos]... the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name.' Parakletos means one called alongside to help, comfort, advocate — precisely what Qohelet observes as missing. The Spirit is the answer to the no-comforter cry: the oppressed who have no earthly comforter are given the divine Comforter, the one who stands alongside in their tears.
2: A shadow: 'And I thought the dead who are already dead more fortunate than the living who are still alive.' The death-better-than-life thought — the depth of Qohelet's pessimism about earthly existence. Philippians 1:21-23: 'For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain... I am hard pressed between the two. My desire is to depart and be with Christ, for that is far better.' Paul's 'death is gain' and Qohelet's 'the dead are more fortunate' express similar observations, but from opposite theological positions: Qohelet sees death as escape from oppression; Paul sees death as arrival at Christ. The death-preference is transformed from resignation to anticipation.
3: A shadow: 'but better than both is he who has not yet been and has not seen the evil deeds that are done under the sun.' The not-yet-born as most fortunate — the extreme form of the pessimism. Revelation 1:18: 'I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades.' Christ holds the keys to the condition that Qohelet describes. The 'not-yet-born' that Qohelet envies exists only in the realm of potentiality; Christ's resurrection makes it possible for those who have seen evil deeds to enter a life that is better than not having been born — life with him beyond death. The preference for non-existence is answered by the living Lord who has conquered the condition that made existence so grievous.
4: A shadow: 'Then I saw that all toil and all skill in work come from a man's envy of his neighbor. This also is vanity and a striving after wind.' Envy as the driving force of human competitive achievement — the motive that makes much human industry vanity. Mark 15:10: 'For he perceived that it was out of envy that the chief priests had delivered him up.' The envy Qohelet identifies as the engine of human striving reaches its darkest expression in the envy that drove the crucifixion of Christ. The competitive vanity-dynamic Qohelet observes in human life produced the murder of the one who was truly fruitful and wise.
5: A shadow: 'The fool folds his hands and eats his own flesh.' The self-consuming laziness of the fool. John 6:53-54: 'Truly, truly, I say to you, unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you. Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life.' The fool 'eats his own flesh' — self-consuming, ultimately self-destructive. Christ offers his flesh to be eaten — not self-consumption but the reception of the life-giving gift. The contrast is between the fool turning inward on himself and Christ turning outward in self-giving. The eating of flesh is the same action in both cases; the direction (inward destruction vs. outward gift) is opposite.
6: A direct connection: 'Better is a handful of quietness than two hands full of toil and a striving after wind.' The handful-of-quietness observation — rest is worth more than frantic productivity. Matthew 11:28-30: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.' Christ offers the 'handful of quietness' that Qohelet commends as better than two hands of anxious toil. The rest that Qohelet observes to be more valuable than striving is what Christ offers not as a passive absence but as the positive rest of living in his easy yoke.
7: A shadow: 'Again, I saw vanity under the sun: one person who has no other, either son or brother, yet there is no end to all his toil, and his eyes are never satisfied with riches, so that he never asks, 'For whom am I toiling and depriving myself of pleasure?' This also is vanity and an unhappy business.' The utterly alone toiler — no companion, no heir, endless unsatisfied striving. Mark 15:34: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The cry of dereliction is Christ entering the ultimate aloneness — forsaken by the Father at the moment of bearing sin. The solitary toiler of Ecclesiastes 4:7-8 whose eyes are never satisfied and who has no companion is the image Qohelet uses for the vanity of aloneness; Christ entered that very aloneness to bear the abandonment that sin produces.
8: A theme: 'Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.' The two-together principle — the better reward of companionship. John 17:21: 'that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us.' Christ's high priestly prayer reframes the two-better-than-one principle as a theological reality: the unity of believers with each other and with the Trinity is the ultimate 'two are better than one.' The companionship that makes toil productive and rewarding is grounded in the Trinitarian communion that Christ shares with the Father and invites his people into.
9: A shadow: 'For if they fall, one will lift up his fellow. But woe to him who is alone when he falls and has not another to lift him up!' The lifter of the fallen companion. Hebrews 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them' — Christ as the eternal companion who never falls and always intercedes for those who do fall. 1 John 2:1: 'if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.' The advocate who lifts the fallen is not a human companion but the risen Christ who intercedes perpetually. The 'no one to lift him up' that Qohelet identifies as the tragedy of aloneness is addressed by Christ's permanent advocacy.
10: A shadow: 'Again, if two lie together, they keep warm, but how can one keep warm alone?' The warmth of companionship — the isolated person cannot maintain warmth. John 10:3-4, 14-16: 'I am the good shepherd. I know my own and my own know me.' The intimate union of shepherd and sheep — the shepherd who calls his own by name, goes before them, and they know his voice — is the NT form of the keeping-warm-together principle. The isolated person who cannot keep warm alone is given the Good Shepherd who dwells with his flock; the Spirit who dwells within (John 14:17: 'he dwells with you and will be in you') is the warmth of companionship internalized.
11: A shadow: 'And though a man might prevail against one who is alone, two will withstand him — a threefold cord is not quickly broken.' The strength of solidarity — two resist what one cannot; three more so. John 17:21-23: 'that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us... I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly one.' The threefold cord — Father, Son, Spirit — is the ultimate unbreakable bond; believers woven into this Trinitarian union are not quickly broken. The sociological wisdom of the threefold cord becomes in Christ's prayer the theology of Trinitarian union extended to include the church.
12: A type: 'Better was a poor and wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knew how to take advice.' The poor-and-wise-youth who is better than the established foolish king — the unexpected inversion of power and status. Philippians 2:7-9: 'he emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men... he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him.' The poor-wise-youth who becomes king is a type of Christ: born in poverty and humility (Luke 2:7: no room in the inn; John 1:46: can anything good come out of Nazareth?), rejected by the established authorities, ultimately exalted above every name.
13: A direct type: 'for he went from prison to the throne, though in his own kingdom he had been born poor.' Prison to throne — the definitive inversion narrative. Philippians 2:8-9: 'he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name.' Ephesians 4:8-10: 'When he ascended on high he led a host of captives... (in saying, 'He ascended,' what does it mean but that he had also descended into the lower regions, the earth?).' The one who descended to the lowest point (the cross, the grave) is the one who ascended to the highest place — the prison-to-throne trajectory fulfilled in the death-and-resurrection of Christ. This is one of Ecclesiastes' clearest pre-figurations of the incarnation-and-exaltation pattern.
14: A shadow: 'I saw all the living who move about under the sun, along with that youth who was to stand in the king's place.' The universal following of the new king — all the living follow the one who takes the throne. John 12:32: 'And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.' The drawing-of-all-people to the lifted-up Christ is the fulfillment of the all-the-living-following-the-new-king image. Where Qohelet's youth-king attracts the multitude transiently (and the next generation will not rejoice in him either, Ecclesiastes 4:16), Christ's drawing is permanent: his 'all people' includes every generation, and the joy of those who come to him is not temporary.
15: A shadow: 'yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and a striving after wind.' The transience of popular adulation — even the greatest leader is forgotten by the next generation. John 6:66-68: 'After this many of his disciples turned back and no longer walked with him. So Jesus said to the twelve, 'Do you want to go away as well?' Simon Peter answered him, 'Lord, to whom shall we go? You have the words of eternal life.'' Christ experienced the abandonment Qohelet predicts — disciples turning back (like all popular leaders eventually experiencing declining followers). But Peter's confession breaks the pattern: 'to whom shall we go?' The twelve are the remnant who do not follow the transience-trajectory; their loyalty is based on something other than popular enthusiasm.
16: A shadow: 'There was no end to all the people, all of whom he led. Yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and a striving after wind.' The final vanity verdict on even the most successful human leader — all popular movements are temporary. John 15:16: 'You did not choose me, but I chose me and appointed you that you should go and bear fruit and that your fruit should abide.' The relationship between Christ and his disciples reverses the leader-popularity dynamic: they are not followers who chose him and will eventually stop rejoicing; they are chosen, appointed, and their fruit is designed to 'abide' — to be permanent. The transience that attaches to every human leader does not attach to Christ, whose chosen ones bear abiding fruit.
